channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.gamedeveloper.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.gamedeveloper.com

item.title: Epic Games layoffs serve as a timely reminder that accountability is dead
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

March 25, 2026

3 Min Read

Yesterday, Epic Games announced it would be cutting over 1,000 jobs because Fortnite isn't quite making as much money as it used to. That's despite Epic CEO Tim Sweeney acknowledging the free-to-play shooter remains "one of the most successful games in the world" in a memo sent to employees. 

The news has raised incredulous eyebrows. Fortnite is perceived as the blueprint for those desperately searching for that glimmering pot of gold at the end of the live service rainbow. 

The company continues to secure more branding collaborations than a TikTok influencer in full, transcendental swing. For instance, it netted a whopping $1.5 billion from Disney in 2024 to build an "all-new games and entertainment universe." Whatever that means.

Epic hasn't disclosed how much revenue Fortnite is pulling in, but recent estimates from Statista indicate it generated around $6 billion in 2025. That is quite a big number—and yet, seemingly too small for Epic to justify the livelihoods of 1,000 people.

Related:Sony shutters internal studio Dark Outlaw Games

Yesterday, an Epic spokesperson told Game File the company will have around 4,000 employees on its books once it has finished carving through its workforce. Who is being put to the sword, however, remains unclear.

When I approached Epic yesterday for clarification on the fate of internal studios Harmonix and Mediatonic—makers of Rock Band and Fall Guys, respectively—I received a decidedly vague response.

"The teams at Harmonix and Mediatonic have been integrated into the Epic development team for a while," said an Epic representative. "They work on a range of games and experiences, and the Harmonix team continues to work on music features in Fortnite like Festival Main Stage and Festival Jam Stage. Music remains a major part of Fortnite."

Both Harmonix and Mediatonic were acquired by Epic Games in 2021. At the time, the company said Harmonix was purchased to "reimagine how music is experienced, created, and distributed." Mediatonic, meanwhile, said Epic would support its mission of building video games that have a "positive impact" on the world. 

Two years later, Mediatonic was reportedly decimated by layoffs after Epic decided to make around 900 people redundant. Now, a flurry of posts on LinkedIn indicate that a number of Harmonix employees have also been cut loose by their parent company. How many? Your guess is as good as ours. It appears those staffers have been assimilated or eliminated. 

It's yet another example of how conglomeration leads to obfuscation and decay. Harmonix and Mediatonic (whose website is riddled with 404 error pages) might still exist in some nebulous form within Epic, but the company's refusal to candidly comment on the status of those studios and the employees it so proudly acquired five years ago only raises more questions than answers. 

Related:Behaviour Interactive acquires 7 Days to Die dev The Fun Pimps

What remains of them now? What should those people who swerved the latest round of cuts expect from their employer? What's going on at Epic-owned Rocket League developer Psyonix? Or, perhaps more pertinently, why even bother asking?

Earlier today, IGN reported that some senior staffers who survived Epic's latest purge are uncertain where the company goes from here. "Our teams will have to pick up the pieces and try to keep moving forward," wrote Fortnite gameplay producer Robby Williams on social media. "But we cannot even fully understand what kind of impacts this will have on the game for the rest of the year and likely beyond."

Employees left scrambling. Players left wondering. Tim Sweeney sarcastically dismissing AI concerns when announcing massive job cuts. All while one major question lingers overhead: is anybody going to take accountability?

I suspect you already know the answer.

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/epic-games-layoffs-should-remind-you-that-accountability-is-dead
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
